title: Learning to Swim
summary: A teenage boy talks about learning to swim at a local pool. He describes his lessons, how he felt at first, and how he improved over time.
body: I started swimming lessons six months ago. Before that, I could not swim at all. My dad took me to the local pool every Saturday. My swimming teacher is called Mr. Harris. He is very patient and kind. On my first lesson, I was very nervous. The water felt cold and I was scared to put my face in. Mr. Harris showed me how to kick my legs and move my arms. I practised in the shallow end of the pool. After a few weeks, I could float on my back. That felt amazing! Then I learned the front crawl. It was hard at first. I swallowed some water and I coughed a lot. But I did not give up. I kept practising every week. Now I can swim twenty metres without stopping. My dad is very proud of me. I feel more confident in the water. Swimming is now one of my favourite activities.

annotations.exampleEn: She signed up for swimming lessons at the local pool.
A good teacher is patient with beginners.
He was nervous before his first swimming lesson.
She kicked her legs and moved forward in the water.
Beginners always start in the shallow end of the pool.
She relaxed and floated on her back in the calm water.
The front crawl is the fastest swimming style.
He swallowed some water when a wave hit him.
She found it difficult, but she did not give up.
Her parents were proud of her when she passed the exam.
After many lessons, he felt more confident in the water.
She ran five kilometres without stopping.

grammarNotes.title: Past Simple – Irregular verbs in a learning story
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài này dùng nhiều động từ bất quy tắc ở thì quá khứ đơn: start → started (thông thường), feel → felt, show → showed (thông thường), learn → learned (thông thường), swallow → swallowed, keep → kept, begin → began. Đây là những động từ rất thường gặp khi kể về quá trình học tập.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: This passage uses many verbs in the simple past to describe a learning journey. Key irregular forms: feel → felt, show → showed, learn → learned, keep → kept. Pay attention to how the past simple is used consistently to narrate events in sequence.

grammarNotes.title: Could / could not – Past ability
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Could' là dạng quá khứ của 'can', dùng để nói về khả năng trong quá khứ. 'I could not swim at all.' = Trước đây tôi không biết bơi chút nào. 'I could float on my back.' = Tôi đã có thể nổi ngửa. Dùng 'could' để nói khả năng đã có, và 'could not' để nói khả năng chưa có trong quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Could' is the past form of 'can'. It is used to talk about ability in the past. 'I could not swim at all' = I was not able to swim at that time. 'I could float on my back' = I was able to float. Use 'could' for past ability and 'could not' (couldn't) for past inability.

translationVi: Tôi bắt đầu học bơi sáu tháng trước. Trước đó, tôi không biết bơi chút nào. Bố tôi đưa tôi đến hồ bơi gần nhà mỗi thứ Bảy. Giáo viên dạy bơi của tôi tên là ông Harris. Ông ấy rất kiên nhẫn và tốt bụng. Trong buổi học đầu tiên, tôi rất hồi hộp. Nước cảm thấy lạnh và tôi sợ khi cho mặt xuống nước. Ông Harris chỉ cho tôi cách đạp chân và vẫy tay. Tôi luyện tập ở phần hồ nông. Sau vài tuần, tôi có thể nổi ngửa. Cảm giác thật tuyệt vời! Sau đó tôi học kiểu bơi tự do. Lúc đầu rất khó. Tôi nuốt nước vào và ho rất nhiều. Nhưng tôi không bỏ cuộc. Tôi tiếp tục luyện tập mỗi tuần. Bây giờ tôi có thể bơi hai mươi mét mà không dừng lại. Bố tôi rất tự hào về tôi. Tôi cảm thấy tự tin hơn trong nước. Bơi lội bây giờ là một trong những hoạt động yêu thích của tôi.

questions.prompt: When did the narrator start swimming lessons?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'I started swimming lessons six months ago.'
questions.hint: How long ago did the lessons begin?

questions.prompt: Could he swim before his lessons?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Before that, I could not swim at all.'
questions.hint: What does 'not at all' mean about his swimming ability?

questions.prompt: Who took him to the pool?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'My dad took me to the local pool every Saturday.'
questions.hint: Which family member went with him?

questions.prompt: What is the swimming teacher's name?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'My swimming teacher is called Mr. Harris.'

questions.prompt: How did the narrator feel on his first lesson?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'I was very nervous' and 'I was scared to put my face in.'
questions.hint: What two negative feelings does he describe?

questions.prompt: Where in the pool did he practise first?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'I practised in the shallow end of the pool.'
questions.hint: Which part of the pool is safer for beginners?

questions.prompt: What could he do after a few weeks?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'After a few weeks, I could float on my back.'
questions.hint: What early skill did he learn?

questions.prompt: What swimming style did he learn next?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Then I learned the front crawl.'
questions.hint: What stroke is described as the next step?

questions.prompt: What happened when he was learning the front crawl?
options: He swallowed some water and coughed
questions.explanation: The passage says 'I swallowed some water and I coughed a lot.'
questions.hint: What physical problem did he have?

questions.prompt: How far can he now swim without stopping?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Now I can swim twenty metres without stopping.'
questions.hint: What distance is mentioned as his current achievement?

questions.prompt: How does his dad feel about his progress?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'My dad is very proud of me.'
questions.hint: What positive emotion does his dad feel?

cloze.template: I started swimming lessons six {{b1}} ago. Before that, I could not swim at all. My dad took me to the local pool every {{b2}}. My swimming teacher is called Mr. Harris. He is very {{b3}} and kind. On my first lesson, I was very nervous. The water felt cold and I was scared to put my face in. Mr. Harris showed me how to kick my legs and move my arms. I practised in the {{b4}} end of the pool. After a few weeks, I could float on my {{b5}}. That felt amazing! Then I learned the front {{b6}}. It was hard at first. I swallowed some water and I coughed a lot. But I did not give {{b7}}. I kept practising every week. Now I can swim twenty metres without {{b8}}. My dad is very proud of me. I feel more {{b9}} in the water. Swimming is now one of my favourite {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'Six months ago' — the time when the narrator began his lessons.
'Every Saturday' — the day his dad took him to the pool.
'Very patient and kind' — qualities of a good teacher for nervous beginners.
'The shallow end' — the safer, less deep part of the pool for beginners.
'Float on my back' — lying face-up on the water surface.
'The front crawl' — a swimming stroke where you move your arms forward alternately.
'Did not give up' — to not stop trying even when something is difficult.
'Without stopping' — swimming the full twenty metres in one go.
'More confident' — the opposite of nervous; feeling sure of one's ability.
'Favourite activities' — things he enjoys doing regularly.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says he swallowed water and coughed, but he did not give up. Why is it important to keep trying when learning something new is difficult? What would you do if you found a skill very hard to learn?
